summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Interim relocation – Where the father seeks to relocate the children’s primary place of residence to Region RR – Where the proceedings have a protracted and complex history – Where two of the parties’ three children require a change of school – Where the children live primarily with the father – Orders made for the parties’ youngest child to spend supervised time with the mother – Orders made permitting the father to relocate the children’s primary place of residence on an interim basis.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

SIMPSON J:

These parenting proceedings, concerning the children X (aged 16), Y (aged 14) and Z (aged 10), have a long history. The Applicant, Mr Bolkvadze (“the father”), and the Respondent, Ms Bolkvadze (“the mother”), have been involved in family law litigation since 2022.
The first round of parenting proceedings culminated in final orders made by Christie J on 24 February 2025 (Bolkvadze & Bolkvadze (No 3) [2025] FedCFamC1F 107). Pursuant to those orders, the children lived with the parents on a week about basis and the father held sole decision-making authority with respect to health and medical matters concerning the children.
Pursuant to s 65DAAA of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act’), and by consent, the parents agreed to reconsider the final parenting orders made by Christie J. That consent position was reached on 6 August 2025, when the matter came before me for interim hearing. Following that interim hearing, I made orders and delivered reasons on 11 August 2025 (Bolkvadze & Bolkvadze (No 4) [2025] FedCFamC1F 535), with respect to the outstanding interim parenting matters then before the court.
Pursuant to those orders made on 11 August 2025, the week about live with orders were suspended. I ordered that the children spend time with the mother during the day only, on Wednesday and each second week, for four hours on Saturday and on Sunday. The older children, X and Y, were at liberty to spend that time and communicate with the mother in accordance with their preferences. I also restrained the mother from contacting the various health providers of the children.
On 14 November 2025 (sealed on 18 November 2025), the father filed a further Application in a Proceeding seeking to change the spend time orders such that Z would spend time every second weekend with the mother, but with that time to be supervised by a professional service, at the mother’s cost. The father also sought an order which would see the father relocate the children’s primary place of residence to Region RR and the enrolment of the children in schools in that area.
A Child Impact Report was released on 24 November 2025. The Child Impact Report dated 24 November 2025 (Exhibit 5), was prepared by Ms XX (“the Child Impact Report writer”).
An interim hearing with respect to the father’s Application in a Proceeding occurred on 10 December 2025. The mother appeared in person, the father was represented by counsel, and the Independent Children’s Lawyer (“the ICL”) also appeared.
A detailed description of the complex litigation history for this family is set out in the reasons of Christie J and in the reasons I delivered on 11 August 2025.

RECENT EVENTS AND THE CURRENT PROPOSALS

In September 2025, the mother attended the father’s home without invitation (while he and the children were present) and entered into his back yard and a studio located there. The mother did not leave, despite the father’s direction that she do so. The police later attended at the father’s request. Each of the children were aware of their mother’s actions (either by direct observation or as a result of telephone discussions between them).
The mother remained outside the father’s home for several hours while she waited for the police to attend. Her mother and sister arrived and waited with her for some of that time.
The father later communicated to the mother that he would not continue to facilitate Z spending time with her because of those events. The mother was subsequently charged and an interim no contact Apprehended Domestic Violence Order was made (in September 2025) for the father’s protection. The criminal charges against the mother have not yet been heard.
The mother’s attendance at the father’s home occurred in circumstances where she had formed the view that the father was controlling, interfering with and, or, disrupting her access to digital resources, and remotely “hacking” and controlling her devices and digital footprint. On the mother’s account, this interference has resulted in complete loss of utility of her telephone and internet services and ability to access online resources.
The bases for the mother’s contention that she has been the victim of such a comprehensive and debilitating cyber-attack are difficult to comprehend from her evidence. The circumstances which lead the mother to conclude that the father is responsible for these attacks (howsoever characterised) also remain unclear. The mother’s contentions in this regard were before the court at the time of the last interim hearing, and in her most recent evidence and additional written submissions, her views and conclusions appear to have solidified.
The father denies any part in creating or causing the technology difficulties about which the mother complains. The police attended the father’s home in August 2025, apparently following upon a complaint made by the mother and questioned the father about the accusations the mother had made. They also conducted a welfare check on the children at that time.
The police attended a second time in October 2025 (the father was not at home) and spoke with the father later. Certain accusations, made by the mother, about the father breaking into her home and changing light globes, were discussed. The father denied the conduct alleged.
As stated previously, the father seeks orders that the spend time orders made on 11 August 2025 be varied and that the mother have supervised daytime contact with Z every second weekend. The father also seeks orders that would enable him to move with the children to live in a location in Region RR and consequent to that move, for the children to attend schools in that location from 2026.
The father sets out in his written evidence his observations that Y has been bullied at his school and that Y has long held a preference to go to a different school. The father says in his affidavit that Y is eager to move to Region RR.
Similarly, the father identifies challenges that Z has experienced at school, particularly with respect to the attitude of his teacher and more broadly the attitude of the principal of the school (as to the issues identified as impacting Z and more generally, to the father and his engagement with the school).
The father says that it is in the best interest of each of the boys that they are enrolled in different schools from 2026. Because of that need, the father has identified schools in the Region RR area which he considers to be suitable for all the children. The need for the change of school, as identified by the father, has added impetus to his renewed application to move to Region RR.
In support of that application, the father contends that he is experiencing financial pressures living in Sydney and that he would prefer to live in Region RR where he understands that he and the children will have an improved standard of living, he will have more affordable rental and, or, may have the opportunity to buy a home. The father also deposes to his preference to live in that location with his partner, who would also prefer to live in that area.
The father’s counsel submitted that the question of an interim relocation (to Region RR) was inherently entwined with the change of school for the boys. While it was submitted that the court is normally reluctant to support an interim relocation (consistent with well-established authority, including Morgan & Miles [2007] FamCA 1230 (“Morgan & Miles”)), in this case, where the evidence suggested the best interests of two of the children required an urgent change to their schooling, there were strong arguments to favour the new schools being located in Region RR.
The mother opposes the orders sought by the father. In her Response filed 9 December 2025 (the filing of which has since been voided), the mother proposes that interim orders be made which provide for her time with Z to occur every second weekend, from 12.00 pm on Saturday until (an unspecified time on) Sunday evening. There is no dispute that the order for mid-week time may be discharged. Similarly, the mother does not seek interim orders about the living or spend time arrangements for the two older children.
The mother submitted that it is premature to determine the father’s relocation application at this time, on an interim basis, especially in circumstances where that same application was made and refused at the final hearing before Christie J. In her case outline document, the mother also submitted opposition to the father’s proposed relocation “in circumstances where the father is likely to be investigated for criminal activity which, if convicted, could require jail time and full care [of the children] with the mother”. There is no evidence to suggest that the police have an ongoing investigation into the father. There are no criminal charges laid against the father.
On 11 August 2025, an ICL was appointed (Order 10). The ICL met with the children on 1 December 2025.
The impact upon the children of the continuing litigation between their parents is considerable.
The ICL submitted however, that it was in the best interests of the children to defer consideration of the change of school dispute until January 2026, when she expected further information could be made available about the school or schools under consideration. That approach was not supported by the father and was not directly addressed by the mother.

DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON

The father relied upon:
(a) His Application in a Proceeding filed 14 November 2025 (sealed 18 November 2025); and

(b) His affidavit filed 14 November 2025.

The mother relied upon:
(a) Her Response to Initiating Application filed 9 December 2025;

(b) A Notice of child abuse family violence or risk filed 9 December 2025; and

(c) Her affidavit sworn/affirmed on 8 December 2025 and filed 9 December 2025.

A range of documents were made exhibits throughout the course of the interim hearing. I have read and had regard to each of those documents. The father’s case outline document became Exhibit 1, the ICL’s, Exhibit 4, and the mother relied upon two case outline documents, one filed 9 December 2025 and which became Exhibit 2, and an earlier case outline document which became Exhibit 3. I made clear to the mother that I would only have regard to those portions of her documents relating to the parenting matters (some of her documents included submissions relating to financial matters). I permitted the mother to rely upon a document titled “Detailed Narrative”, which was received by email on the day of the hearing and which became Exhibit 22.

ISSUES TO BE DETERMINED

The ICL in her case outline nominated the issues to determined as follows:
(a) Whether the father should be permitted to relocate to Region RR with the children; and

(b) Whether the mother’s time with the children should be supervised.

To those above issues, I would add:
(a) Whether the children Y and Z require enrolment in different schools for 2026. The need to change schools for the boys is one of the factors which the father submits, creates circumstances which warrants the court considering his application to live in Region RR on an interim basis. The father contends that a change of school for Y and Z is necessary and in their best interests, regardless of whether the family remain living in Sydney or if they live in Region RR. The children presently attend schools in Suburb YY.

THE LAW

When making parenting orders, the best interests of the children remains the paramount consideration, and I am required to consider the matters set out in s 60CC of the Act, as follows:
General considerations
(2) For the purposes of paragraph (1)(a), the court must consider the following matters:
(a) what arrangements would promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of:
(i) the child; and

(ii) each person who has care of the child (whether or not a person has parental responsibility for the child);

(b) any views expressed by the child;

(c) the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child;

(d) the capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs;

(e) the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so;

(f) anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the child.

(2A) In considering the matters set out in paragraph (2)(a), the court must include consideration of:
(a) any history of family violence, abuse or neglect involving the child or a person caring for the child (whether or not the person had parental responsibility for the child); and

(b) any family violence order that applies or has applied to the child or a member of the child’s family.

I am also to apply s 60CG of the Act, which provides:
(1) In considering what order to make, the court must, to the extent that it is possible to do so consistently with the child’s best interests being the paramount consideration, ensure that the order:
(a) is consistent with any family violence order; and

(b) does not expose a person to an unacceptable risk of family violence.

(2) For the purposes of paragraph (1)(b), the court may include in the order any safeguards that it considers necessary for the safety of those affected by the order.

This is an interim hearing and there has been no cross examination of the parties, which creates limitations as to any findings which can be made.
I have regard to the guidelines recommended by the Full Court with respect to the hearing of interim applications where there may be little agreed or uncontroversial evidence: Goode & Goode (2006) FLC 93-286. In Franklyn & Franklyn [2019] FamCAFC 256, the Full Court said:
In interlocutory hearings, to the extent it is possible, judges are enjoined to make decisions about interim orders based on agreed facts, less contentious evidence, and inferences which fairly arise (see Goode and Goode (2006) FLC 93-286 at [68]), but decisions must still be made despite contentious evidence. Judges are obliged to act on the strength of the evidence presented and, if it is relatively weak, are entitled to treat it so. Contrary to the inherent premise of the mother’s submissions in support of Ground 2(c), judges are not required to treat all untested evidence as bearing the same weight.
Where there are allegations of risk made by a party (relating in this matter to mental health vulnerabilities), the evidence about those serious issues cannot be ignored, just because a finding about those disputed matters cannot be made at this time.
In Marvel & Marvel (No 2) [2010] FamCAFC 101; (2010) 43 Fam LR 348, the Full Court said:
As has frequently been emphasised interim parenting proceedings, and orders made as a consequence, are a necessary but temporary measure until all the evidence can be tested, evaluated and weighed at a final hearing by the making of final parenting orders. Decisions judicial officers have to make in interim proceedings are difficult and, often for very good reason, a conservative approach, or one which is likely to avoid harm to a child is adopted. This is often to the understandable distress of a party who may not achieve the outcome he or she desires, or thinks to be in the best interests of their child or children. Interim parenting orders are frequently modified or changed after a final hearing, and any allocation of parental responsibility made at an interim hearing is disregarded at the final hearing (s 61DB).
...
In SS & AH [2010] FamCAFC 13 the majority (Boland and Thackray JJ) discussed at paragraph 88 of their reasons the care necessary to be exercised in making findings in interim parenting proceedings. Their Honours said:
In our view, findings made at an interim hearing should be couched with great circumspection, no matter how firmly a judge’s intuition may suggest that the finding will be borne out after a full testing of the evidence.
123. Later, at paragraph 100 their Honours amplified their comments and said:
The intuition involved in decision-making concerning children is arguably of even greater importance when a judge is obliged to make interim decisions following a hearing at which time constraints prevent the evidence being tested. Apart from relying upon the uncontroversial or agreed facts, a judge will sometimes have little alternative than to weigh the probabilities of competing claims and the likely impact on children in the event that a controversial assertion is acted upon or rejected. It is not always feasible when dealing with the immediate welfare of children simply to ignore an assertion because its accuracy has been put in issue.
The Full Court in Deiter & Deiter [2011] FamCAFC 82, observed as follows:
The assessment of risk is one of the many burdens placed on family law decision makers. Risk assessment comprises two elements – the first requires prediction of the likelihood of the occurrence of harmful events, and the second requires consideration of the severity of the impact caused by those events. In our view, the assessment of risk in cases involving the welfare of children cannot be postponed until the last piece of evidence is given and tested, and the last submission is made. We accept, however, that it is always a question of degree depending on the evidence that is before the Court.
It is accepted that “relocation cases” are not a separate subset of parenting proceedings and that the same principles apply to the resolution of these parenting disputes, within the usual framework of the best interests of the children. Applications made on an interim basis, to change the location of where the children live (or to give the court’s imprimatur to a change which has already occurred on an interim basis) create additional challenges. I have regard to the observations of Boland J (sitting as the Full Court) in Morgan & Miles, that the court should be reluctant to determine issues of relocation on an interim basis:
It appears to me that the very difficult issues in cases involving a relocation, which difficulties are highlighted in the cases and referred to by the Family Law Council in its 2006 report Relocation: a report to the Attorney-General prepared by the Family Law Council, (Family Law Council of Australia, Barton, 2006) make it highly desirable that, except in cases of emergency, the arrangements which will be in the child’s best interests should not be determined in an abridged interim hearing, and these are the type of cases in which the child’s present stability may be extremely relevant on an interim basis. It further appears to me the comments of Warnick J in C and S remain apt and relevant to determination of these cases.

CONSIDERATION OF THE EVIDENCE

The events of [...] September 2025

Each of the parties provides an account of the events of [...] September 2025, when the mother attended the father’s home, unannounced, when the father and the children were there. Even on the mother’s account, it is undisputed that the father did not react with anger towards her and that he directed himself to the mother respectfully and calmly. Given the children were home, the father’s restraint was important and protective of them.
Despite that, it is clear from the children’s discussions with the Child Impact Report writer that these events have impacted them. X said she was “a little bit worried” about her mother when she came to her father’s home and described her mother’s behaviour as “silly”. X reported that her mother said she was “hacked” and remained at the home, until the police attended (Exhibit 5 at [13]). The father deposes to X refusing to speak with the mother when she telephoned on 27 September 2025.
Y described his mother “yelling about someone breaking into her home” and that she had accused his father of doing this. He described these events as “scary” and expressed his concerns about his mother’s behaviour, which he did not perceive as “normal” (Exhibit 5 at [22]). Y also spoke to the Child Impact Report writer of other matters impacting his current preference about not spending time with his mother. He indicated that he would be willing to spend time with her during the day but has not done so “as he is concerned that his mother has not changed or acknowledged her behaviour” (Exhibit 5 at [25]). These comments by Y are made in the context of Y living with the father since May 2025, following certain events which occurred at the mother’s home when Y had been in her care. Those events are set out in the reasons I delivered on 11 August 2025, commencing at paragraph 30.
Z suggested to the Child Impact Report writer that he was uncertain as to why he was not presently spending time with his mother. The Child Impact Report writer indicated that Z presented as not wanting to be seen to choose one parent over the other (Exhibit 5 at [26]). He described a positive relationship with his mother and did not identify concerns about her behaviour and that he felt safe with her. He said similarly positive things about his father (Exhibit 5 at [29]).
The father reported to the Child Impact Report writer that Z was present when the mother attended the home and that “[Z] was distressed and continued to be distressed about his mother’s behaviour” (Exhibit 5 at [32]).
Of these events, the Child Impact Report writer reports the mother’s account as follows:
[Ms Bolkvadze] during her interview acknowledged that she attended [Mr Bolkvadze’s] home without consent, but she appeared to minimise and justify her behaviour as she believed that [Mr Bolkvadze] had stolen her belongings. [Ms Bolkvadze] alleges that [Mr Bolkvadze] has engaged in technology abuse by writing false emails to the children’s medical professionals from her, hacking her devices, monitoring her movements through technology and stealing her belongings from her home and she believes that he has engaged in such behaviour since 2022. [Ms Bolkvadze] said that she has reported her concerns to police but no further action has been taken.
The Child Impact Report writer identifies certain concerns with respect to the mother’s emotional state and mental health functioning.
This assessment raises concern about [Ms Bolkvadze’s] current emotional state. [Ms Bolkvadze] during her interview appeared to be distressed, her speech at times was not clear, she stuttered and appeared to have difficulties communicating and she moved from different topics. [Ms Bolkvadze] displayed some ideation that could be considered as delusional and significantly paranoid and she appeared to be unable to shift from these views. There is other collateral information that raise concern about [Ms Bolkvadze’s] mental health and how this may be impacting her parenting capacity. However, it is not within the scope of this assessment to assess her mental health. It is possible that [Ms Bolkvadze] has been a victim of cyber abuse but there is significant concern about how [Ms Bolkvadze’s] rigidity in her views and her behaviour is impacting her parenting capacity and her relationships with the children. [Ms Bolkvadze’s] apparent rigid views about the children’s health needs and her apparent inability to accept the professional opinion and treatment plans of the professionals currently engaged with the [children] raises concern about her parenting capacity.
...
[Ms Bolkvadze] presented as a distressed parent and it seems that she is experiencing grief and loss regarding her relationships with the children. However, this assessment raises concern about [Ms Bolkvadze’s] parenting capacity and her insight into her own behaviour. There is concern that [Ms Bolkvadze] deflects and places blame on [Mr Bolkvadze] rather than also reflecting on how her own behaviours has potentially negatively impacted her relationships with the children. It is also acknowledged that [Ms Bolkvadze] was previously a primary caregiver to the children, and it may be that engaging in ongoing litigation as well her communication with [Mr Bolkvadze] may pose as a significant stressor to her that has negatively impacted her emotional wellbeing. However, regardless of which is the case, there still remains concern about how this is impacting her behaviour and her relationships with the children as a result.
The mother submitted that she understood that she had “made a bad choice” when attending the father’s home.
In her Notice of child abuse, family violence or risk document filed on 9 December 2025, the mother asserted the children were at risk of family violence in the father’s care. In setting out the details of the alleged harm or risk of harm (ostensibly relating to drug, alcohol or substance misuse, given the information is provided at question 23, though drug or alcohol use did not feature in the narrative at that part) the mother states of her own conduct:
... when she later attempted to recover stolen possessions from the father’s office, she was charged [...]. Her action must be understood in the context of ongoing domestic violence, coercive control, and the theft of her identity documents the previous day...This charge illustrates how the father’s conduct has escalated to criminalising the mother’s attempts to protect herself and recover her property, further silencing her and destabilising her life.
As the above extract suggests, the mother holds to a view that the father has engaged in “identity theft” and has stolen property from her and that his conduct (in seeking police assistance when she attended his home) is an “escalation”.
Elsewhere in that document (in response to question 28) the mother makes further assertions as to the father’s conduct, including that he has:
... weaponised institutions against the mother. He has made false reports to police, DCJ and health professionals, and has interfered with her communications by impersonating her through spoofed emails and dummy account. He has also attempted to silence her by spreading false claims of mental illness to family and community members.
The mother produces no evidence to support her contentions that the father has engaged in the conduct addressed above. These statements evince an absence of insight as to her own actions and also suggest that the mother views the father through a distorted or disordered lens, inconsistent with the evidence of what occurred and her responsibility for these events.
It is clear from the mother’s court documents that she holds the father responsible for the fact that she has been charged because of her conduct in September 2025.

Breakdown in the spend time arrangements between the children and the mother

X has had some limited time with her mother since the last orders were made. However, the father deposes to some uncertainty and unpredictability around these arrangements.
The father deposes to difficulty in securing the mother’s adherence to the time frames for her time with the children, as set out in the orders of 10 August 2025, with the children being returned late during the week (impacting the evening and bedtime routines) and late on weekends.
Y has not spent time with his mother since he came into his father’s care in May 2025. Y identified to the Child Impact Report writer that he does not feel safe with his mother; that her moods can change from yelling at him to acting with care towards him, making him feel confused. He also spoke of the conflict he experienced around her views of his medication requirements (Exhibit 5 at [19]). There were other matters reported by Y to the Child Impact Report writer about aspects of his mother’s engagement which he found challenging, including her discussing his father and the court proceedings with him. He remained reluctant to spend time with his mother but would like to repair his relationship with her (Exhibit 5 at [21]).
The father also asserts that the mother did not communicate with Z between 16 August and 23 October 2025 and that there was some irregularity to the time the mother spent with Z under the August 2025 orders.
The father suspended the mother’s time with the children after the events at his home in September 2025.

Difficulties for Y and Z at school

Y described to the Child Impact Report writer his views of his school, including that he has (only) one friend at school, that he has difficulties with some of his peers because he does not agree with their “misogynistic” attitudes towards women and he distances himself from these peers (Exhibit 5 at [17]). Y, who has been diagnosed with ADHD, anxiety and has a provisional diagnosis of Avoidant-Restrictive Food Intake Disorder (“ARFID”), is currently not on medication, as advised by his paediatrician (Exhibit 5 at [18]). The nature and extent of medication for Y has been a source of contention between the parents for some time, notwithstanding the father having sole responsibility for decisions about these matters.
The Child Impact Report writer records that Y supported the idea of living in Region RR as “the area is cheaper and that he prefers the weather and scenery there” (Exhibit 5 at [25]). Y was noted as being “not concerned about having to change schools as he said that he only has one friend at school and does not feel aligned with his peers” (Exhibit 5 at [25]).
The father describes Y’s experiences at school as more serious than non-alignment with his peers, instead asserting that Y is being bullied. The father deposes to Y being very enthusiastic about the possibility of moving to Region RR.
Y attends upon a psychologist, and the father acknowledges that the psychologist has been working with Y on strategies to assist in response to the bullying. Exhibits 12, 13 and 14 were session summaries relating to Y’s attendance upon his psychologist in October, November and September 2025 respectively. Exhibit 14 (being a session in September 2025) primarily addressed how Y was feeling after his mother had come to the home. He described being sad about these events but not surprised by them.
Exhibit 12, being the notes of the session in October 2025, records Y’s reports of bullying at school, including various groups (presumably of students) telling Y “just kill yourself” and refusing to sit near him or to engage with him. Other instances of the students urging Y to kill himself are noted. Y is noted as advising the psychologist that he wants to change schools and he would like to move to Region RR and go to school there.
Other options rather than changing schools are explored in the session, including engaging with the school about the behaviour of the other students. Y was given strategies for how to respond to the bullying behaviour.
In November 2025 (Exhibit 13), Y is seen again by his psychologist. The record notes “bullying still present” and that Y has explored options with his father but still does not want to talk to the school as he does not think it is likely to help. The document records that Y did not want the psychologist to contact the school.
Z has also reported difficulties at school. The father describes Z as experiencing anxiety about his teacher “for much of this year” and he reports that he has been in trouble and shouted at regularly by the teacher. The father deposes to steps he has taken this year to attempt to secure the assistance of the school in supporting Z. The father’s attempt to access counselling services at the school were thwarted because, allegedly, the mother would not agree to the counselling and then (even after the children were living primarily with him) the principal would not support the referral to the counsellor.
The evidence available currently suggests that the principal of the school has formed a view about Z’s stated experiences of his teacher, which view is inconsistent with Z’s reports to his father. The father states that he does not feel supported by the principal in his endeavours to support Z, including while the father worked through a period of school refusal by Z.
The father’s evidence about his experience of engagement with the principal (including a lack of response to matters he has shared with her) suggests that the relationship has broken down. The mother has advised the father that the principal has “reported” the father to the Department of Communities and Justice (“DCJ”), and the father has been copied into emails sent by the mother to the principal in which the mother makes allegations with respect to the father’s approach to Z’s medical care (about which he has sole decision-making authority).
The father deposes to Z’s anxiety increasing after the mother’s attendance at the home in September 2025, and while getting Z to school was previously “effortful”, after those events, the father says that Z was unable to attend school for full days. The father deposes to changing his work commitments to be available to assist Z and to engage in homeschooling with him when he could not attend school.
The father has continued to support Z’s attendance upon his own psychologist, and issues around his school experiences have been a focus of more recent sessions. The father arranged a meeting with Z’s psychologist and the school principal to assist in managing the child’s anxiety and to support his school attendance. Despite that meeting occurring on 3 November 2025, the father asserts that the school did not take steps to implement the discussed plan and did not discuss the changes with Z (at the time of swearing the father’s affidavit on 14 November 2025).
Exhibit 10 is a note of a discussions between Z’s psychologist and the school principal, in September 2025. The note suggests that the principal reported she had made a report to the DCJ because of her view of the father’s “controlling behaviour towards school, dictating whether mum can pick him up”. The notes suggest that the principal also expressed “significant concerns” about the father “gaslighting” the school about Z’s attendance. The note records that the psychologist advised the principal that these matters (and the father’s responses) have been shaped by Z’s preferences.
The father deposes to attending a psychologist appointment with Z in October 2025 when Z agreed with the psychologist that he was “open to changing schools”. After that meeting, the father facilitated the school principal participating in discussions with him and Z’s psychologist. Exhibit 11 is a note made by the psychologist of a discussion between the psychologist, the father, and the principal, in November 2025. At that time, it is noted that Z had been attending school “partially” for five weeks but was ready to do a full day. The principal is recorded as stating that Z’s “chronic absence” and non-attendance was a concern for the school.
The document concludes that there was a “transition plan for new school”.
Z reported to the Child Impact Report writer about his school experience, that he does not enjoy school as much because the teacher “favours particular students”. Z told the Child Impact Report writer that he did not want to move to Region RR as he did not want to change schools, move to an unfamiliar area, nor to be further away from his mother (Exhibit 5 at [30]).
The father identifies in his evidence that X is likely to be most impacted by a move to Region RR, given she is reasonably content at her school and has made progress in her ability to engage at school and develop friendships. X acknowledged to the Child Impact Report writer that she has had better school attendance while in her father’s care (that he encouraged her attendance), that she would rather any move away be deferred until after she had finished school and that she might have both her parents nearby to support her through the final years of school (Exhibit 5 at [16]).
X also attends upon a psychologist. Exhibit 8 was a note from X’s attendance with her psychologist in December 2025. X reported having visited schools in Region RR with her family and she and her brother, Y, had both indicated their preference for a particular school. X had been engaged in a work experience placement over the previous couple of weeks which she thought may have impacted upon her ability to see her mother. The document suggests that X reported not having been able to communicate with her mother via an app and via mobile (she had attempted to communicate with her mother but had not received responses). The document records X “expressing mild concern about her mother’s wellbeing”.
X is reported as acknowledging conflicting feelings about her living arrangements – while she would prefer equal time with her mother and her father, she acknowledged that she experiences better functioning (particularly with respect to school attendance and exam performance) when with her father.
X reports seeing her mother attend the father’s home in September 2025 and describes her mother’s actions as “stupid”.
X is reported as expressing that scheduled visits with her mother would be helpful for maintaining contact and providing certainty.

The views of the children

The ICL submitted, as a consequence of her discussions with the children, that:
(a) Y was “manifestly unhappy” in his school and that he is being bullied at his school;

(b) That X is settled at her school and does not want a change to occur;

(c) More generally, both boys were unhappy in their schools; and

(d) The children were very aware of the continuing conflict and ongoing court proceedings between their parents.

X has indicated, as set out above, that she understands why living with her father has assisted her in achieving better school engagement. She has expressed to her psychologist her preference for a week about arrangement but can see that she does do better at managing school when with her father. X expressed a preference for regular time to be spent with her mother.
As noted by the Child Impact Report writer, X understands why her father is interested in a move to Region RR but would prefer it occurred later. However, it appears from her discussions with her psychologist (Exhibit 8) that X understands the benefits her father identifies in living in that location and she is more reconciled to the possibility of such occurring. X has identified a particular school in that area which she and her brother Y have agreed is better than others they had considered.
Y is now 14 years old. He is very keen to move to another school and appears to be supportive of the idea of moving to Region RR. The evidence makes clear that Y’s school experience is not positive and he reports being bullied at his school.
A change of school for Y appears to be necessary given that the strategies explored by his psychologist with him about those issues have not resulted in improvement of outlook for Y. He reports having no confidence in his school to entrust them with assisting him to find solutions and has refused to engage with the school in that fashion.
Z said to his psychologist that he would prefer to return to a 50/50 care arrangement (and his preference about this is repeated by the other children). Z has been vulnerable to the absence of regular, predictable time with his mother and remains uncertain as to why he is not spending time with her.
Z is however experiencing challenges at his school and has felt unsupported by the school in addressing the difficulties he has had with his teacher.

Risks relating to the mother’s emotional state and mental health

The Child Impact Report writer expressed several concerns with respect to the mother’s presentation and her allegations relating to the father and his conduct. The Child Impact Report writer queried the mother’s current mental health and wellbeing (in the paragraphs extracted previously).
The mother has provided some limited information about her current engagement with her psychiatrist. Exhibit 23 was a letter dated September 2025 addressed “to whom it may concern” from the mother’s treating psychiatrist Dr VV. The mother submitted that the letter was prepared by Dr VV to assist the mother in her engagement with Centrelink. The letter does not provide information as to Dr VV’s assessment of the mother but merely repeats information provided (it appears) to Dr VV by the mother, explaining her inability to engage with certain requirements of Centrelink, due to “multiple stressors related to court case around family and children custody and being in DV relationship”.
The mother’s engagement with police over several months also resulted in observations by the police as to her ongoing mental health fragility. This was confirmed by the contents of Exhibit 26, being the first page of the NSW Police Force COPS record for the events in August 2025, when it appears the mother made certain reports to the police about her views of certain of the father’s actions. That document notes of the mother that she appeared visibly distressed and alleged that her ex-partner had hacked her accounts and was gaining access via a device shown to police. The device presented to police “was identified as a standard Wi-Fi dongle, which does not possess the capability to perform such actions...”.
That exhibit also contains the equivalent record (being the first page only) relating to events in October 2025 when the police have attended the mother’s home and have spoken with her. The record includes observations by the police of concerns that the mother may have mental health issues. The final page in that exhibit is the first page of a NSW Police Force COPS entry for October 2025, relating to the progress of the police inquiries arising from the mother’s earlier engagement with them. The document includes a statement by a police officer to the effect of her concern that the mother may have mental health issues and that:
She ranted at me by phone for 20 minutes about how her ex-husband is able to control all phones and can track her all times. She believes there has been a further break in today but could not provide any information to me other than a bin at the front of the premises had been moved and documents stolen.
In October 2025, the police attended the mother’s home following her reports that someone had broken into her home. The NSW Police COPS report for that event was Exhibit 7. The document records that the mother “appeared to be paranoid and heightened”. The document includes that the mother “displayed erratic emotional behaviour, including abruptly walking away during conversation and crying without reason...” and a view is expressed that the mother may have been experiencing a mental health episode, and a referral was made to mental health supports.
The mother endorsed that the police had made a referral for her to access certain mental health services.
In circumstances where the mother’s presentation in court was at times highly labile, she was at times distressed and dysregulated, I gave the mother additional time to produce to the court the information which she said she could access from the mental health team, to which she had been referred by the police. A direction about this was made when the hearing concluded. After an additional extension of time, the mother produced (on 15 December 2025) a discharge report from the Community Health Centre at the hospital to which she was referred. That document became Exhibit 30 and related to an assessment of the mother in October 2025.
The document notes that two telephone calls with the mother occurred, in October 2025 and “there were no acute psychiatric concerns” assessed. The document records that the mother denied “symptoms of a major mood disorder or psychotic features” and “denies suicidal ideation, thoughts of self-harm or homicidal ideation”. The document notes that the mother “reports domestic violence as her main concern” and she was encouraged to continue to engage with domestic violence services.
Little assistance as to the mother’s ongoing mental health status can be drawn from that document summarising a superficial engagement by a Nurse Practitioner with the mother (noting that the only discussions occurred over the telephone) and which largely depends upon and repeats the mother’s self-report.
Review of the mother’s written evidence itself is suggestive of some difficulties in her functioning and her perception of matters, which appear quite improbable (even on the mother’s account) but to which the mother appears to adhere with certainty and rigidity. In particular, the mother’s allegations as to the father’s cyber-hacking and technology abuse of her are difficult to follow, expressed with little coherent particularity and the documentation produced by the mother in an attempt to prove her contentions is unhelpful.
The court does not have the benefit of a report from the mother’s treating psychiatrist or an independent psychiatrist as to the mother’s current mental health functioning.

Should Z’s time with the mother be supervised?

There are a number of considerations which weigh in favour of supervision of the time which the mother may spend with Z.
The Child Impact Report writer identified aspects of the mother’s emotional state as suggestive of delusional and significantly paranoid ideation (Exhibit 5 at [43]). In addition, the Child Impact Report writer identified the mother’s persistent and rigid views as to the children’s health needs and her inability to accept the opinions of the treating professionals as raising “some concerns about her parenting capacity” (Exhibit 5 at [43]).
The Child Impact Report writer identified some risks to Z that unsupervised time “might expose [Z] to psychological harm particularly if his mother were unable to be child focussed and continued to be rigid in her views regarding the children’s needs and their father” (Exhibit 5 at [48]).
The father also identifies some risk in the children being exposed to the mother’s rigid thinking about the cyber-attack she asserts she has experienced and her theories about this, including the source of the attack. The mother’s conduct in coming to the father’s home in September 2025 is suggestive of her difficulty in regulation and maintenance of boundaries.
The mother’s conduct in attending the father’s home in September 2025 has impacted each of the children. Her lack of insight (despite accepting that she made a bad choice) and understanding as to the problems created by her actions (and her persistent blaming of the father) suggests that there remains some risk that the children may be exposed to her views about the father and his actions, if her time with the children was not supervised.
The Child Impact Report writer expresses concern as to the impact upon the emotional wellbeing of the children of the disruption of their relationship with the mother. The father says that the mother’s time with the children, as ordered in August 2025, was not adhered to by the mother and both Z and X experienced disruptions to that regime of time (for a number of reasons, including the failure by the mother to avail herself of all of the time available to her and on other occasions, the mother unilaterally extending time beyond that provided in the orders).
The ICL identifies some risk that the imposition of supervision may act as a further barrier to Z resuming time with his mother, in circumstances where Z misses his mother and craves the resumption of regular time with her. However, the mother has already had the benefit of orders for defined, unsupervised day time with Z and the evidence suggests that she has been unable to adhere to the parameters of those orders. There are additional consequences for Z, as identified by the father, when the mother returns him late from time with her – including the pressure created around mealtime for the family. This is in circumstances where management of Z’s engagement in family meals is part of the recommended response to his provisional ARFID diagnosis and disruption to mealtime may impact upon his nutritional intake. The father sets out in his affidavit examples of other disruption and changes to Z’s time with the mother, under the August 2025 orders, sought or imposed by the mother. This includes the mother cutting short some of her visits with Z.
If supervised time occurs, through a professional service, regular sessions will need to be planned and booked in advance. Having a regime of supervised time may restore some structure and routine given the process required to establish the service and secure dates and times. Subject to the mother’s adherence to that schedule, this will create additional welcome structure for Z. It is possible that if such was in place, X might also elect to join Z in spending time with her mother, given she wants the restoration of regular time and communication with her mother. X is in any event, at liberty to make other arrangements direct with her mother (and such arrangements have been supported by the father in the past).
In interim proceedings, where the evidence of the parties has not been tested, I must proceed with some caution. However, aspects of the mother’s conduct and her reports as to the father’s behaviour appear to be bizarre and are certainly not properly based in evidence she has presented to the court to substantiate her accusations. Given the mother’s lack of insight as to the impact and consequences of her poor choices and the bizarre and unsubstantiated allegations she makes about the father (and which appear to pre-occupy her and are of considerable focus for her), I have some concerns about risk for the children in having unsupervised time with the mother.
Supervision of the mother’s time with Z will ensure that he is not exposed to the mother’s highly critical views of the father about his conduct or other unusual or paranoid views more generally. The time that X may spend with her mother may coincide with the spend time arrangements put in place for Z, or she may make other arrangements direct with the mother. X’s comments to the Child Impact Report writer suggest that she wants to spend regular time with her mother. Y also appears to seek time with his mother, subject to certain qualifications (including about her attitude to certain matters important to him). Given X’s and Y’s age and the agreement between the parties that they are at liberty to spend time and communicate with the mother in accordance with their wishes, I do not propose to require that time to be subject to supervision. The older children are less vulnerable to the impact of any inappropriate behaviour by the mother and are likely to elect to suspend time with her or otherwise make changes to those arrangements if they experienced them to be uncomfortable. Z is younger and would benefit from arrangements which provide some independent oversight while securing the resumption of regular spend time arrangements with his mother.
I do not have evidence as to the mother’s current mental health functioning. The Child Impact Report writer identifies her concerns as to the mother’s presentation at the time of the interviews on 19 November 2025.
Y’s report to the Child Impact Report writer suggests that he has been exposed to aspects of the mother’s views and functioning which he found difficult (prior to May 2025 when he resolved to live with his father only). There is some risk that the mother’s functioning has deteriorated further (the events of September being suggestive of a deterioration in her ability to regulate herself and adhere to boundaries expected in day-to-day engagement).
Neither parent is presently proposing the continuation of mid-week time between the mother and Z. On that basis, the imposition of supervision will not impact the regularity of the mother’s time with Z, even on her own application.
These are the second interim proceedings before the court for these children since February 2025 and the evidence is suggestive of deterioration of the mother’s general functioning and engagement with the children. That consideration weighs in favour of a more cautious approach being taken to the arrangements for the children’s time with their mother.
Another benefit of supervision of the mother’s time with Z is that each parent can receive independent information about the progress of the time. This may assist the parties in reaching future agreement and may hasten the return to unsupervised time if these arrangements were to progress well and without incident.
I recognise that supervised time is not without challenge for a child who wants to spend time with their parent and who may resent the limitations around supervision. It creates some limitations to what the parent may do with their child (even if the service supports time occurring in the community) and will remove much of the spontaneity that is otherwise available when time occurs. I place some weight on those considerations, especially given Z’s desire to spend time with, and communicate with, his mother and his uncertainty as to why that is not occurring.
I accept that the mother opposes the imposition of supervision. However, the evidence is such that the factors which favour the imposition of supervision and the need to act protectively of Z, while fostering his important relationship with his mother, outweigh the mother’s preference to have unsupervised time with the child and the limitations, identified above, about supervised time.
The father sought orders that the mother meet the costs of supervision, if ordered. The father presently meets all of the financial needs of the children. The mother is not employed and the father receives no child support from the mother.
The father deposes to ongoing financial difficulties, some of which he says are the product of the high costs of living along with some disruption to his work in order to more fully care for the children, reducing his ability to take on additional clients (he is a health professional).
I have regard to Exhibit 19, being a trust statement from the father’s lawyers, dated 10 December 2025, evidencing funds held in that account on behalf of the mother. Those funds are the product of the final property settlement orders made by Christie J in February 2025. The mother has, it appears, declined to receive those funds. While the mother did not address these matters in her affidavit, the mother submitted that she has a “financial application” pending. That submission appears to be misguided. I note that there is no extant appeal from the final property orders made by Christie J. In her Response dated 9 December 2025, the mother sought final orders which included reference to the difficulties in assessing the property pool and “costs order sought for expense of legal proceedings commenced without full and frank disclosure”. In the interim orders she sought in that document, the mother referred to re-opening the final property orders and seeking orders under s 79A of the Act (relating to “material non-disclosure, falsification of records and fresh evidence now available”). As noted previously, that Response has been voided and removed from the court’s document system.
For the reasons set out above, I will accede to the father’s application that the mother’s time with Z should be subject to professional supervision. I will also order that the mother should meet that cost. I am persuaded that while the mother may not presently be in employment, she may access the funds held on her behalf as evidenced in Exhibit 19 and on that basis, has some resources from which this cost may be met. The imposition of the requirement of supervision is protective of Z and the maintenance of his relationship with the mother, creating the framework for regular and predictable time with his mother. The father, however, already meets the financial needs of the children and it is appropriate in these circumstances that the mother makes this contribution to the maintenance of this relationship.

Interim relocation

If the only issue to be determined on an interim basis was the father’s preference to live in Region RR, long established authority would weigh against favouring that application on an interim basis (instead, leaving that issue to be determined at a future final hearing when all of the evidence could be properly tested). Although, the father does not need to show compelling reasons why he wishes to live in another place, he has set out in his affidavit clear evidence of the factors which he identifies as favouring a move and those which he identifies as weighing against a move.
The question of whether it is in the best interest of these children to consider, on an interim basis, the father’s application to change their place of residence, is inextricably linked to the question of whether a change of school for Y and, or, for Z is in their best interests at this time. It is contended on behalf of the father, that each of the boys need to be enrolled in different schools for 2026, and that on that basis, it is less disruptive for them to facilitate their enrolment at schools in Region RR now, given it is the father’s position that the children should live with him in Region RR on a final basis. The father contends that if he is required to find alternate schools for the boys in the Suburb YY area, the children may then face another move to a different school, in the event that they in due course move to live with him in Region RR.
Usually, one of the considerations which favours deferral of the consideration of an interim relocation is the benefit to the children of protecting stability in their present living circumstances (see Morgan & Miles). However, the uncontroversial evidence in this matter suggests that the children have since mid-2025, experienced change, uncertainty and pressures upon their care arrangements and for different reasons, each of the boys now requires a change of schooling.
The children have gone from living with their parents on a week about basis, to Y refusing to spend time with his mother (since May 2025), and the reduction in the time Z has with his mother to daytime only, as a product of the hearing in August 2025. In that same period, X’s time with her mother, which it was agreed would occur subject to her views and preferences, has become limited and sporadic and has been subject to uncertainty. This is in part, it appears, a product of the difficulty that the father and the children have experienced in attempting to communicate with the mother (which she explains as the product of the cyber-hacking and digital violence she says has been undertaken by the father).
None of the children presently communicate with the mother with regularity. The father has suspended the arrangements for Z to spend time with the mother, following her unilateral and unwelcome attendance at his home in September 2025.
The evidence makes clear that Y is very unhappy in his present school and he has a long held and consistent desire to leave that school. Y is 14 and his reports to the Child Impact Report writer, as evidenced in the notes from his attendance with his psychologist and as confirmed in his discussions more recently with the ICL, make clear that he requires a change of school. Y reports being bullied and has rejected any benefit to attempting to secure better outcomes with the input of his current school.
The father accepts Y’s reports about his unhappiness at that school and seeks orders which would make it possible for Y to attend another school (and has nominated his preferred school in Region RR).
The mother contends that if Y needs to change school, he can attend another school local to his home in Suburb YY but did not provide evidence directed to these issues. The mother has not identified a suitable alternate school for Y. The interim orders she sought in her Response were silent about the schooling issue for each of the boys save that she sought an order for her to have “SPR for education and medical for all three children”.
Z has experienced difficulties at school this year too and had a period of significant school rejection, following the events of September 2025 (which is not to say that those events necessarily caused his refusal to attend school, but they are temporally linked). Z has expressed some ambivalence about changing his school, but the evidence makes clear that the steps taken by the father to secure help and support of Z from the school have been unsuccessful (including the failure of the school to make counselling services available to the child). By November 2025, it was agreed between the father and the principal of the school that Z would transition to another school.
The mother does not accept that it is in Z’s interests to change school and she contends that the father seeks the change because of his poor relationship with the principal, rather than because of the child’s needs. However, while the cause of the breakdown of the relationship between the adults may be presently unclear, the evidence at this time suggests that Z’s continuing enrolment at that school is not tenable.
No alternate school is identified by the mother as being suitable for Z.
For each of Y and Z, if they were to be enrolled in other schools local to their home in Sydney, they may then face a further change in the event a change of residence was ultimately to be ordered at a final hearing.
The evidence makes clear that X would be content to remain at her school and has identified the progress she has made this year both at school and with respect to her social supports and independence. X also identifies that she has been able to engage and attend more consistently with school while living with her father.
Exhibit 8 suggests that X has become more sanguine about the prospects of a move to Region RR and changing schools, and has identified the benefits for her brothers and father in such occurring.
These are unusual circumstances. Two of the subject children will benefit from and need to be enrolled in different schools this year. The parents are not in agreement about these matters.
I am persuaded that a change of school for each of the boys is necessary and is in their best interests. The father has nominated schools in Region RR/Town WW area which he says will best meet the needs and interests of the children. In the unusual circumstances of this case, I will make the orders as sought by the father, enabling the children to attend the local Public School in Region RR (and the school nominated appears to accord with X and Y’s preferences). I accept that the father has made sufficient inquiries about the various schools and has identified the school which he says will best meet the needs and interests of the children. Because of this decision, I will discharge Order 7 of the Orders of 24 February 2025 (about the children’s schooling).
A consequence of that order will be that the children will live with the father, in a location proximate to those schools.
In making that decision, I have regard to the matters addressed previously in these reasons and the following:
(a) The father is meeting the day to day and financial needs of the children with no financial support from the mother. He sets out in his written evidence details of the financial stressors he experiences in attempting to maintain the children’s accommodation and support generally, in their present location in Sydney. Those pressures include paying high rent and impacts upon his earning capacity when the children’s needs require his closer practical assistance (reducing the number of clients he can take on from time to time). The father says that he is drawing upon capital to meet the costs of his household.

(b) The children have a range of additional needs, and the father has engaged a range of medical and therapeutic service providers to assist the children. This includes facilitating the children attending regular appointments in person and others over telehealth.

(c) He also sets out the practical limitations of supporting each of the children to attend school and engage fully in their communities, given the particular needs of the children. The father identifies the need to live proximate to the schools attended by the children to best facilitate each of them attending school and I accept these challenges would be more acute in the event the two boys were attending other schools in the Suburb YY area. The father identifies the practical challenges and pressures of supporting the children to get to and from school and the “juggle” this creates for him, while maintaining his employment.

(d) The father continues to engage private lawyers, and I have regard to the costs he is incurring in the continuation of these proceedings. The costs of his legal representation in these proceedings are another contributor to his financial difficulties. The mother is self-represented.

(e) The father hopes to share a home with his partner and has identified the challenges of finding suitable and affordable rental in the Suburb YY area, in the event they formed a combined household. He identifies better and more affordable options for a larger home in Region RR, still with proximity to his preferred schools.

(f) The father does not wish to live in the city. He is the primary carer of the children and has expressed a desire to live in another location. The right of a parent to live in a place of their choosing is important and should be supported unless such conflicts with the best interests of the children.

(g) The mother correctly submits that the question of the possible relocation of the children to Region RR was the subject of contested final proceedings and the father’s application about that was ultimately rejected by Christie J. However, the circumstances which informed the February 2025 Orders have changed (as recognised by the parties given their agreement to revisit the parenting orders, confirmed in August 2025). The children no longer live in a shared care arrangement across the two households of their parents. The mother’s engagement with and care of the children has reduced since the final orders were made in February 2025. Y spends no time with his mother. X’s time is subject to her preferences but her engagement with her mother has been quite limited. In the intervening period, it has been necessary to restrain the mother from contacting and communicating with the children’s medical providers and her rigid attitude about those matters has been identified by the Child Impact Report writer as of some concern.

(h) Because of orders I have made, the mother’s time with Z will now occur every second weekend (and will be subject to professional supervision). That regular time arrangement can be supported in the event the children live in Region RR and the children living in that location does not meaningfully limit the ability of the children to maintain their relationship with their mother.

(i) I note that X would like to spend more time with, and communicate with, her mother. That may occur even if the parties are living in different locations. The father continues to express his support of the relationship between X and her mother. Each of the parents may be required to take additional steps to ensure that opportunities for X to spend time with her mother may be facilitated.

(j) I have accepted the father’s contention that a change of school is necessary for Y and for Z. In circumstances where the children (in differing circumstances) spend limited or no time with the mother, I am not persuaded that it is their best interests that the father should be denied the opportunity to move to Region RR until a final hearing. Deferral of consideration of these issues to a final hearing places a continuing burden of uncertainty upon the children (and the father) and they have already experienced the burden of extended engagement in family law proceedings. The older children are very aware of the conflict between their parents and the continuing involvement in court proceedings.

(k) In her Response (which was relied upon the mother in the interim hearing but the filing of which has since been voided), on a final basis, the mother proposed orders that Z would live primarily with her and that the older children would spend time with her in accordance with their wishes. As stated previously, the mother sought interim orders providing for her fortnightly weekend care of Z. Accordingly, the final proceedings (if a final hearing is required) will focus upon the care arrangements for Z only.

(l) I am not persuaded that consideration of where the children live should be deferred until a final hearing given the manner in which the mother has engaged in these proceedings to date and given the primary parenting dispute relates to the living arrangements for one child only (that is, the care arrangements for the older two children do not appear to be controversial).

(m) The father is now responsible for the primary care of the children and is tasked with meeting all of their day-to-day needs. He has no practical assistance from the mother and has no financial assistance from her. His responsibilities include attendance to the myriad additional needs of each of the children, including with respect to their medical care (including supporting and meeting the cost of their engagement with medical specialists and psychologists). I am not persuaded that it is in the best interests of these children that their father should be required to continue to meet these responsibilities in circumstances which he says, make it harder for him to meet his obligations to the children. I remain concerned that to defer the consideration of the move to Region RR until a final hearing will impose upon the father additional pressures which may then impact his ability to meet the needs of the children.

(n) The mother’s written evidence and ancillary documents do not illustrate an ability to focus upon issues relevant to the parenting matters in dispute between the parties. Her engagement is suggestive of a decline in her mental health functioning and certain risks relating to her parenting capacity have been identified by the Child Impact Report writer.

(o) It is in the best interests of these children that the proceedings between their parents in this Court conclude as soon as possible.

I will make orders in accordance with the application of the father. In doing so, I note that this outcome does not necessarily reflect the views and preferences of all the children. The preferences of the children are not the same and I recognise that X did not want to change school or move at this time (preferring to defer that prospect for another two years) and that Z wants more time with his mother. Y however is keen for, and enthusiastic about, changing schools and needs such to occur given his adverse experiences at school in 2025. In circumstances where two of the children will need to change schools this year and where the father identifies more general benefits which will flow to the children in the event the family moves to Region RR, on balance, I am satisfied these changes will more readily meet the best interests of all of the children.
The spend time arrangements between Z and the mother (and as agreed between the parties, for X and or Y with the mother) should also continue during school holiday periods, unless otherwise agreed and the orders will make that clear. The parties have been able to agree upon facilitating the children spending time with the maternal family for Christmas 2025 and these orders will not preclude other additional time arrangements being agreed between the parents, including for other maternal family events (such as birthday celebrations).
I will discharge Order 4 of the orders made on 11 August 2025 and set out in these orders the regime of per fortnight, weekend time for the mother to spend with Z (for term and holiday periods, unless otherwise agreed between the parties). I expect that in doing so, it will be useful to the parties to have the current spend time arrangements expressed in one set of orders, limiting the need for cross referencing.
I have also discharged Order 6 of the orders of 24 February 2025 (special occasion time) in circumstances where that was sought by the father. I will instead incorporate orders for the mother to spend time with Z on special occasions, subject to such being professionally supervised or supervised by a lay person, as agreed between the parties. There is no reason why Z should not experience some additional time with the mother on these occasions, to the extent the adults can make the necessary arrangements for supervision. While this may include time in the mid-week, the mother is not working and I expect will have opportunity to make sure she is able to be local to the children on these occasions, should she wish to do so. The dates of those events are also known with certainty and arrangements for supervision can be made well in advance. I have set out that each such occasion, save for Mother’s Day which will occur on Sunday, be for a period of up to three hours, noting that the requirements of supervision may necessitate a shorter period of time and time which accords with the operating hours of the service provider.
The father has an ADVO for his protection and which precludes the mother communicating with him. However, Order 23 of the orders of 24 February 2025 also provides for communication between the parties. The father alleges that he had received communication from the mother which he says was in breach of the provisions of the ADVO. The father deposes to referring those matters to the police and the police advising him that the pre-existing order of this court permitting communication between the parties prevailed. However, it would be of benefit to the children to ensure that the communication between the parents remains respectful and directed to matters relating to the care and welfare of the children only. In these circumstances, I will make an additional order which will make clear that any communication between the parties is to be limited to matters relating to the care and, or, welfare of the children. To the extent of conflict or inconsistency with the ADVO, pursuant to s 68Q of the Act, this revised communication order will prevail.
The ICL also sought orders about the parties each providing information to the ICL about the medical or other treaters upon whom they have attended in the last 12 months. To the extent that the parties have not yet provided this information to the ICL, I include an order to this effect.
I propose to re-list the matter on a date to be set in April 2026. This will enable time for the children to be enrolled and settled in their new schools and hopefully by that next date, Z will be having regular time with his mother. I will also give liberty to the ICL to seek to relist the matter upon seven days’ notice, in case she holds concerns about the implementation of these orders or more generally.
In the event the matter requires another final hearing, directions as to the preparation for such can be made on the next occasion.
